title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access and modify the instance variables of an object in Smalltalk, such as the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time`, you need to use accessor methods. In Smalltalk, instance variables are private to the instance itself, and there is no direct language syntax to access another object's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined (Pharo By Example, p. 89).

Here is how you can achieve this:

1. **Define Accessor Methods in Class1**: First, you need to define getter and setter methods for the `year` instance variable in `Class1`. This allows other objects to access and modify the `year` variable.

```smalltalk
Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear
```

2. **Use Accessor Methods in Class2**: Once the accessor methods are defined, you can use them in `Class2` to set the `year` variable of the `start-time` object.

```smalltalk
Class2>>initialize
    start-time := Class1 new.
    end-time := Class1 new.
    start-time year: 2012.
```

In this example, `start-time year: 2012` calls the setter method `year:` on the `start-time` object, setting its `year` instance variable to 2012.

This approach ensures proper encapsulation and adheres to the object-oriented principles of Smalltalk, where instance variables are accessed and modified through methods rather than directly (Pharo By Example, pp. 89, 298-299).

By following these steps, you can effectively access and modify the `year` variable in the `Class1` object `start-time` from within `Class2`.
supporting_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , all instance variables are private to the object that owns them , so the only way for another object to read or write those variables is through accessor methods like this one .']
supported_facts_labels: ["In Smalltalk , instance variables are private to the instance itself , and there is no direct language syntax to access another object 's instance variables unless accessor methods are defined ( Pharo By Example , p. 89 ) .", 'This approach ensures proper encapsulation and adheres to the object - oriented principles of Smalltalk , where instance variables are accessed and modified through methods rather than directly ( Pharo By Example , pp . 89 , 298 - 299 ) .']

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
To define a Pharo class method that takes three arguments, you need to use the correct syntax for keyword messages. In Pharo, keyword messages can take an arbitrary number of arguments, and each part of the method name is followed by a colon and an argument name (p. 63).

Your current method definition has a syntax error because it does not correctly follow the keyword message format. Here is the correct way to define a method with three arguments:

```smalltalk
MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: argValue2 var3: argValue3
```

In this corrected method definition:
- `MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3` is the method signature.
- `argValue1`, `argValue2`, and `argValue3` are the arguments passed to the method.
- The method body uses these arguments to perform its operations.

When you want to call this method, you should use the following syntax:

```smalltalk
ClassName new MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
```

This call sends the message `MethodName:arg2:arg3:` to an instance of `ClassName` with the specified arguments.

Here is an example to illustrate:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: MyClass [
    MyClass >> MethodName: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3 [
        ^ {argValue1. argValue2. argValue3}
    ]
]

| instance result |
instance := MyClass new.
result := instance MethodName: 1 arg2: 2 arg3: 3.
Transcript show: result printString.  "Outputs: #(1 2 3)"
```

In this example:
- `MyClass` is a subclass of `Object`.
- The method `MethodName:arg2:arg3:` is defined to return an array containing the three arguments.
- An instance of `MyClass` is created, and the method is called with the arguments `1`, `2`, and `3`.
- The result is printed to the Transcript.

This approach ensures that your method is correctly defined and can be called with the intended arguments (pp. 63-64).

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
To implement a constructor in Pharo, you need to follow a specific pattern. The issue with your current approach is that you are trying to define an instance method `newName:newAge:` and call it directly on the class, which is not the correct way to define or use constructors in Pharo.

In Pharo, the typical way to create and initialize an object is to override the `new` class method to call an instance method `initialize` where you set up the initial state of the object. Here’s how you can do it:

1. **Define the class**:
    ```smalltalk
    Object subclass: #Person
        instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
        classVariableNames: ''
        category: 'Test'
    ```

2. **Override the `new` class method**:
    ```smalltalk
    Person class >> newName: aName newAge: aAge
        ^ self new initializeName: aName age: aAge
    ```

3. **Define the `initializeName:age:` instance method**:
    ```smalltalk
    Person >> initializeName: aName age: aAge
        name := aName.
        age := aAge.
        ^ self
    ```

4. **Ensure the `initialize` method is called**:
    ```smalltalk
    Person >> initialize
        super initialize.
        "Any additional initialization if needed"
    ```

Here’s a step-by-step explanation:

- **Step 1**: You correctly define the class `Person` with instance variables `name` and `age` (p. 97).

- **Step 2**: You override the `new` class method to call a custom initialization method `initializeName:age:`. This is a common pattern in Pharo to ensure that objects are properly initialized when they are created (p. 93).

- **Step 3**: The `initializeName:age:` method sets the instance variables `name` and `age` and returns the initialized object (p. 37).

- **Step 4**: The `initialize` method is called as part of the object creation process. It’s a good practice to call `super initialize` to ensure that any initialization in the superclass is also performed (p. 104).

Now, you can create a new `Person` object with the desired initial values like this:

```smalltalk
objPerson := Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10.
```

This approach ensures that the object is properly initialized and adheres to the conventions used in Pharo for object creation and initialization.
supporting_facts_labels: ['The initialize method is automatically executed after an object is created in Pharo .', 'By convention , if a class defines a method named initialize , it is called right after the object is created .', 'Important It is a good practice that an initialize method start by sending super initialize .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , the typical way to create and initialize an object is to override the ` new ` class method to call an instance method ` initialize ` where you set up the initial state of the object .', 'The ` initialize ` method is called as part of the object creation process .', "It 's a good practice to call ` super initialize ` to ensure that any initialization in the superclass is also performed ( p. 104 ) ."]

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
The error you are encountering, `MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>>Transcript`, occurs because `Transcript` is not a method of the `OrderedCollection` class. Instead, `Transcript` is a global object used for logging system messages, similar to a system console (p. 16).

To print the contents of an `OrderedCollection` to the `Transcript`, you need to convert the collection to a string representation and then send that string to the `Transcript`. Here is how you can do it:

1. **Convert the OrderedCollection to a String**: You can use a `WriteStream` to build a string representation of the collection.
2. **Send the String to the Transcript**: Use the `Transcript show:` method to display the string.

Here is the corrected code:

```smalltalk
| oc |
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).

Transcript show: oc printString; cr.
```

Explanation:
- `oc printString` converts the `OrderedCollection` to its string representation.
- `Transcript show:` displays the string in the Transcript window.
- `cr` adds a carriage return (new line) after the output.

This approach leverages the `printString` method, which provides a textual representation of the collection, and the `Transcript show:` method to display it (pp. 226-227).

For example, if `oc` contains the elements `2` and `#(4 9)`, the output in the Transcript will be:

```
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
```

This method ensures that the contents of the `OrderedCollection` are correctly displayed in the Transcript without causing any errors.
supporting_facts_labels: ['The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages .']
supported_facts_labels: ['Instead , ` Transcript ` is a global object used for logging system messages , similar to a system console ( p. 16 ) .']

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
In Pharo, there is no special syntax for control constructs like `if`, `elseif`, and `else`. Instead, these constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. To handle multiple conditions, you can chain `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages.

Here is how you can write an `if elseif elseif` construct in Pharo:

```smalltalk
x := 25.

(x = 25)
    ifTrue: [ "Do something if x equals 25" ]
    ifFalse: [
        (x < 25)
            ifTrue: [ "Do something if x is less than 25" ]
            ifFalse: [
                (x > 25)
                    ifTrue: [ "Do something if x is greater than 25" ]
                    ifFalse: [ "Do something else if none of the above conditions are met" ]
            ]
    ].
```

In this example, the first condition checks if `x` is equal to 25. If true, it executes the corresponding block. If false, it proceeds to check if `x` is less than 25, and so on. This chaining of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions sequentially.

This approach leverages the fact that conditionals in Pharo are expressed by sending messages like `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to the result of a boolean expression (Pharo By Example, pp. 66, 83, 195-196).

For example:

```smalltalk
(17 * 13 > 220)
    ifTrue: [ 'bigger' ]
    ifFalse: [ 'smaller' ].
```

This evaluates the expression `17 * 13 > 220` and sends the appropriate message based on the result (Pharo By Example, p. 66).

By chaining these messages, you can effectively create an `if elseif elseif` structure in Pharo.
supporting_facts_labels: ['4.6 Conditionals and loops in a nutshell Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs .', 'Instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers and collections , with blocks as arguments .', 'Some conditionals Conditionals are expressed by sending one of the messages ifTrue : ,', 'Following this principle , the conditional branches of ifTrue : or ifTrue : ifFalse : require blocks .', 'If the block evaluates to true , execution continues .', 'For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , there is no special syntax for control constructs like ` if ` , ` elseif ` , and ` else ` .', 'Instead , these constructs are expressed by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments .', 'To handle multiple conditions , you can chain ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages .', 'This chaining of ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` messages allows you to handle multiple conditions sequentially .', 'If true , it executes the corresponding block .', 'This approach leverages the fact that conditionals in Pharo are expressed by sending messages like ` ifTrue : ` , ` ifFalse : ` , or ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` to the result of a boolean expression ( Pharo By Example , pp . 66 , 83 , 195 - 196 ) .']

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
In Pharo, the `self subclassResponsibility` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes. An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly, and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses. These methods are known as abstract methods.

When you define an abstract method in Pharo, you use the `self subclassResponsibility` expression within the method body. This serves as a placeholder, indicating that the method must be implemented by any concrete subclass. If a subclass does not override this method and it gets called, an exception will be raised, signaling that the subclass should have provided an implementation (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).

For example, consider the `size` method in your `AbstractArrayDictionary` class:

```smalltalk
size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility
```

This method indicates that any subclass of `AbstractArrayDictionary` must provide its own implementation of the `size` method. If a subclass does not implement this method and it is called, an error will be raised, clearly indicating which method was not overridden (Pharo By Example, p. 311).

Using `self subclassResponsibility` is crucial because it enforces the contract that subclasses must fulfill. It ensures that the abstract class remains incomplete and cannot be used directly without providing the necessary implementations in its subclasses. This is a common practice in object-oriented design to define a framework for behavior that subclasses must adhere to (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).

In contrast, if you were to implement the `size` method in `AbstractDictionary` as follows:

```smalltalk
size
"dictionary size"
^self size
```

This would create an infinite recursive call, as the method would keep calling itself without ever reaching a concrete implementation. This is why the `self subclassResponsibility` approach is necessary—it explicitly marks the method as abstract and ensures that subclasses provide the required behavior (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).

Therefore, you should not remove the `size` method from `AbstractArrayDictionary` if it is meant to be an abstract method. Instead, ensure that all concrete subclasses like `SimpleDictionary` and `FastDictionary` provide their own implementations of the `size` method. This maintains the integrity of your class hierarchy and ensures that each class fulfills its intended role (Pharo By Example, pp. 97, 186-187).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Instead , by convention , the body of an abstract method consists of the expression self subclassResponsibility .', '* Return self subclassResponsibility to make a method abstract .', 'An abstract class is a class that exists to be subclassed , rather than to be instantiated .', 'The " placeholder " methods , those that the other methods assume to be ( re)defined are called abstract methods .', 'An abstract class is usually incomplete , in the sense that it does not define all of the methods that it uses .', 'This indicates that subclasses have the responsibility to define a concrete version of the method .']
supported_facts_labels: ['This is why the ` self subclassResponsibility ` approach is necessary -- it explicitly marks the method as abstract and ensures that subclasses provide the required behavior ( Pharo By Example , pp . 97 , 186 - 187 ) .', 'In Pharo , the ` self subclassResponsibility ` method is used to define abstract methods within abstract classes .', 'An abstract class is designed to be subclassed rather than instantiated directly , and it typically contains methods that are meant to be overridden by its subclasses .', 'These methods are known as abstract methods .', 'When you define an abstract method in Pharo , you use the ` self subclassResponsibility ` expression within the method body .', 'It ensures that the abstract class remains incomplete and can not be used directly without providing the necessary implementations in its subclasses .', 'This serves as a placeholder , indicating that the method must be implemented by any concrete subclass .']

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
To address your queries about the syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0, let's clarify the correct approach and concepts:

### 1. Correcting the Syntax for Class Methods

In Pharo, class methods are defined on the class side, and instance methods are defined on the instance side. Your method definition and invocation have some issues. Here’s how you can correct them:

#### Defining a Class Method

To define a class method, you need to ensure it is on the class side. Here’s an example of how to define a class method `createNewUser:name:email:phone:`:

```smalltalk
CreateUser class>>createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
    "Method implementation here"
```
(Source: "Pharo By Example", p. 57-59)

#### Calling a Class Method

To call this class method, you should use the class name followed by the method name:

```smalltalk
CreateUser createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
```
(Source: "Pharo By Example", p. 57-59)

### 2. Understanding Class and Instance Variables

In Pharo, class methods are dynamically-dispatched methods, unlike static methods in Java. This means inheritance, overriding, and super-sends work for class methods in Pharo (p. 92). Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses, similar to instance variables but at the class level (p. 92).

### 3. Accessing Instance Variables

Instance variables in Pharo can only be accessed within the instance methods of the class that defines them or its subclasses. To access these variables from outside, you need to define accessor methods (getters and setters) (p. 89).

#### Example of Accessor Methods

Here’s how you can define and use accessor methods for instance variables:

```smalltalk
CreateUser>>name: aName
    name := aName.

CreateUser>>email: anEmail
    email := anEmail.

CreateUser>>phone: aPhone
    phone := aPhone.
```
(Source: "Pharo By Example", p. 89)

To call these instance methods, you need to create an instance of the class first:

```smalltalk
| user |
user := CreateUser new.
user name: userName.
user email: userEmail.
user phone: userPhone.
```
(Source: "Pharo By Example", p. 89)

### Summary

1. **Class Method Definition and Invocation**:
   - Define on the class side: `CreateUser class>>createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone`
   - Call using: `CreateUser createNewUser: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone`

2. **Class and Instance Variables**:
   - Class methods in Pharo are dynamically-dispatched.
   - Class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses.

3. **Accessing Instance Variables**:
   - Define accessor methods to access instance variables.
   - Create an instance of the class to call instance methods.

By following these guidelines, you should be able to correctly define and use class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0.

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
If your Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, you can break out of the loop by using the keyboard shortcut `CMD-.` (Command and period) to interrupt the execution and open a debugger on the computation (Pharo By Example, p. 140). This will allow you to inspect the state of the program and identify the cause of the infinite loop.

In case the `CMD-.` shortcut does not work, you can bring up an emergency debugger by typing `CMD-SHIFT-.` (Command, Shift, and period) (Pharo By Example, p. 140). This emergency debugger can help you regain control of the image and stop the problematic process.

Once you have the debugger open, you can examine the call stack and identify the methods involved in the infinite loop. You can then terminate the offending process or edit the code to fix the issue. For example, you might insert a `self halt` statement in the suspect method to bring up the pre-debugger, allowing you to proceed or debug further (Pharo By Example, p. 140).

Additionally, it is good practice to save your work frequently. Pharo allows you to save the state of your image, so if you encounter an issue, you can restart from the last saved state without losing significant progress (Pharo By Example, p. 15).

Here is an example of how you might edit a method to include a halt for debugging purposes:

```smalltalk
MyClass>>myMethod
    "Some code that might cause an infinite loop"
    self halt.
    "Rest of the method"
```

By including `self halt`, you can trigger the debugger at a specific point in your code, making it easier to diagnose and fix issues (Pharo By Example, p. 311).

In summary, use `CMD-.` to interrupt an infinite loop and open the debugger, or `CMD-SHIFT-.` for an emergency debugger. Save your image frequently to avoid losing work, and consider using `self halt` in your code to facilitate debugging (Pharo By Example, pp. 15, 140, 311).

title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you typically start by using the class creation template provided in the Pharo environment. This template can be accessed by selecting the desired package in the left-most pane of the browser, which will display a class creation template in the bottom pane (Pharo By Example, pp. 119-120). 

Here is a step-by-step guide to creating a class:

1. **Edit the Class Creation Template**:
   - Replace `Object` with the name of the existing class you want to subclass.
   - Replace `NameOfSubclass` with the name you want to give to your new subclass.
   - Fill in the instance variable names if you know them.

   For example, to create a class `Dog` that subclasses `Object` with an instance variable `count`, you would modify the template as follows:
   ```smalltalk
   Object subclass: #Dog
       instanceVariableNames: 'count'
       classVariableNames: ''
       package: 'TestC'
   ```

2. **Accept the Changes**:
   - After editing the template, accept the changes to create the class. If the `!` symbol appears, it indicates that there are unsaved changes or errors that need to be addressed.

3. **Define Class Methods**:
   - Class methods are defined on the class side and do not require an instance to be created to function. They are similar to static methods in Java. For example, to initialize and increment the `count` variable, you can define the following class methods:
   ```smalltalk
   Dog class >> new
       count := count + 1.
       ^ super new

   Dog class >> count
       ^ count
   ```
   (Pharo By Example, p. 93)

4. **Define Instance Methods**:
   - Instance methods are defined on the instance side and are used when an object of the class is instantiated. For example, you might define a method to return the sound a dog makes:
   ```smalltalk
   Dog >> sound
       ^ 'Woof!'
   ```

5. **Testing the Class**:
   - You can test the class by creating instances and calling the methods. For example:
   ```smalltalk
   | aDog bDog |
   aDog := Dog new.
   Transcript show: Dog count; cr.  "Should print 1"
   bDog := Dog new.
   Transcript show: Dog count; cr.  "Should print 2"
   Transcript show: aDog sound; cr.  "Should print 'Woof!'"
   ```

In summary, creating a class in Pharo involves editing a class creation template, defining class and instance methods appropriately, and testing the class to ensure it behaves as expected. Class methods are defined on the class side and are used for operations that do not require an instance, while instance methods are defined on the instance side and are used for operations on instantiated objects (Pharo By Example, pp. 91-94, 119-120).

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
To generate getter and setter methods automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak, you can leverage the reflective capabilities of Smalltalk. This involves dynamically adding instance variables and their corresponding accessors to a class. Here’s a step-by-step approach to achieve this:

1. **Define a Method to Add Instance Variables and Accessors:**
   You can create a method that takes a class and a list of instance variable names, then dynamically adds these variables to the class and generates the corresponding getter and setter methods.

2. **Dynamic Class Modification:**
   In Pharo, you can modify class definitions dynamically. This involves using the reflective methods provided by the system to add instance variables and compile new methods.

3. **Example Implementation:**
   Here’s an example of how you might implement such a method:

   ```smalltalk
   addVariablesAndAccessors className: aClass variablesList: aList
       aList do: [:varName |
           "Add instance variable to the class"
           aClass addInstVarName: varName.
           
           "Generate getter method"
           aClass compile: varName, ' ^ ', varName.
           
           "Generate setter method"
           aClass compile: varName, ': anObject', String cr, varName, ' := anObject.'
       ].
   ```

   This method iterates over the list of variable names, adds each as an instance variable to the class, and compiles the corresponding getter and setter methods.

4. **Using the Method:**
   You can call this method with your class and list of instance variables:

   ```smalltalk
   addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: #('aaaa' 'bbbb' 'cccc').
   ```

5. **Dynamic Accessor Generation:**
   Pharo also supports dynamic accessor generation using the `doesNotUnderstand:` mechanism. This can be useful if you want accessors to be created on-the-fly when they are first accessed. Here’s an example:

   ```smalltalk
   DynamicAccessors >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
       | messageName |
       messageName := aMessage selector asString.
       (self class instVarNames includes: messageName)
           ifTrue: [
               self class compile: messageName, ' ^ ', messageName.
               ^ aMessage sendTo: self ].
       ^ super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.
   ```

   This method traps any message not understood by the object. If the message corresponds to an instance variable, it dynamically compiles a getter method for that variable and then re-sends the message to itself (pp. 315-316).

By following these steps, you can efficiently generate getter and setter methods for a class in Pharo or Squeak, ensuring that your code remains clean and maintainable while leveraging the powerful reflective capabilities of Smalltalk (pp. 49, 119-120, 298-299, 315-316).

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something every 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you need to ensure that the delay and printing operations are executed sequentially within the loop, rather than forking a new process for each iteration. Forking creates concurrent processes that can lead to all messages being printed at once, as seen in your initial code.

Here is a corrected version of your code:

```smalltalk
| i delay |
i := 0.
delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    delay wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'; cr.
    i := i + 1.
].
```

In this version, the `delay` is created once before the loop starts, and the `wait` message is sent to the `delay` object within the loop. This ensures that the loop waits for 2 seconds before printing the message and then increments the counter. The `Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'; cr.` line prints the message and adds a carriage return after each message.

This approach ensures that the message is printed every 2 seconds, as intended.

For more information on using the `Transcript` and handling delays, you can refer to the following sections in "Pharo By Example":

- Using the `Transcript` for output: "There are also convenient messages for printing useful characters to a stream, such as space, tab and cr (carriage return)" (p. 227).
- Executing expressions in the Playground: "In the Pharo window, click on an open space to open the World Menu, and then select the Playground menu option. The Playground tool will open" (pp. 13-14).
- Iterating with loops: "We can also send the message to:do: to a number which then acts as the initial value of a loop counter" (p. 67).

By following these guidelines, you can ensure that your code prints the message every 2 seconds as expected.

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk, and by extension Pharo, is referred to as a "dynamically typed" language because it does not require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, or method returns. Instead, types are associated with objects at runtime rather than at compile time. This means that the type of an object is determined dynamically when the program is executed, rather than being statically checked by the compiler before execution (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).

In Pharo, nearly all operations are performed by sending messages to objects. For instance, when you perform an operation like `3 + 4`, you are sending the `+` message with the argument `4` to the integer object `3`. The actual method that gets executed depends on the class of the object receiving the message (Pharo By Example, p. 100). This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of Pharo's dynamic typing system.

Moreover, variable declarations in Pharo are not executable statements but merely allocate space for object references. Assignments bind variable names to objects within their scope, and returns simply pass the computed result back to the caller. These operations do not involve static type checks (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).

The dynamic nature of Pharo's type system allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment, often referred to as "live coding," where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation (Pharo By Example, p. 1).

In summary, Smalltalk is called "dynamically typed" because it determines the types of objects at runtime through dynamic message sending and does not require static type annotations, allowing for a highly flexible and interactive coding experience (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).
supporting_facts_labels: ['In Pharo there are only dynamic message sends .', 'Ok , so nearly everything in Pharo happens by sending messages .', 'A live coding environment that allows the programmer to modify code while the code executes , without any need to slow down their workflow .', 'In fact , variable declarations are not even executable .', 'One of the unique features of Pharo ( and its ancestors ) is that while you are programming , you are living in a world of live objects , not in a world of static program text .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , nearly all operations are performed by sending messages to objects .', 'live coding , " where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation ( Pharo By Example , p. 1 )"', "This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of Pharo 's dynamic typing system .", 'Moreover , variable declarations in Pharo are not executable statements but merely allocate space for object references .', 'The dynamic nature of Pharo \'s type system allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment , often referred to as " live coding , " where code can be modified and executed on the fly without the need for recompilation ( Pharo By Example , p. 1 ) .']

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, "instanceVariableNames" and "classVariableNames" serve different purposes and have distinct scopes within a class definition.

**Instance Variable Names:**
Instance variables are specific to each instance of a class. They are defined using the "instanceVariableNames" keyword in the class definition. These variables are private to the instance itself, meaning that each instance of the class has its own set of values for these variables, and they cannot be directly accessed by other instances of the same class. This encapsulation ensures that the internal state of an object is protected and can only be accessed or modified through methods defined in the class or its subclasses (p. 89).

For example, if you define a class `Point` with instance variables `x` and `y`:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #Point
    instanceVariableNames: 'x y'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Geometry'
```
Each `Point` object will have its own `x` and `y` values, and these values can be accessed within the instance methods of the `Point` class or its subclasses.

**Class Variable Names:**
Class variables, defined using the "classVariableNames" keyword, are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. This means that there is only one copy of each class variable, and it is accessible to all instances of the class as well as the class methods. Class variables are useful for storing data that needs to be shared across all instances, such as configuration settings or counters (pp. 109-110).

For example, if you define a class `Dog` with a class variable `count` to keep track of the number of `Dog` instances:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #Dog
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: 'count'
    category: 'Animals'
```
The `count` variable will be shared among all `Dog` instances and can be accessed and modified by class methods to keep track of the total number of `Dog` objects created.

**Key Differences:**
1. **Scope and Accessibility:**
   - Instance variables are private to each instance and can only be accessed within the instance methods of the class or its subclasses (p. 89).
   - Class variables are shared among all instances of the class and the class itself, making them accessible in both instance and class methods (pp. 109-110).

2. **Encapsulation:**
   - Instance variables provide encapsulation at the instance level, ensuring that the internal state of an object is protected from other instances (p. 89).
   - Class variables do not provide the same level of encapsulation since they are shared across all instances and the class itself.

3. **Usage:**
   - Instance variables are used to store data specific to each instance.
   - Class variables are used to store data that needs to be shared among all instances and the class itself.

By understanding these differences, you can effectively use instance and class variables in Pharo to manage the state and behavior of your objects and classes.

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return "self" by default if no explicit return value is specified. This design choice is rooted in the philosophy and practical benefits of the language.

### Philosophical Background
The default return of "self" aligns with the principle that methods should only return a value when it is of interest to the sender. Kent Beck refers to this as the "Interesting return value" principle: "Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value" (Pharo By Example, p. 104). This approach encourages clarity in code, making it explicit when a method's return value is meant to be used.

### Practical Benefits
1. **Method Chaining**: One of the primary practical benefits of returning "self" by default is to facilitate method chaining. Method chaining allows multiple methods to be called in a single statement on the same object, enhancing readability and conciseness. For example:
   ```smalltalk
   myObject doThis; doThat; doSomethingElse.
   ```
   Here, each method call returns the receiver, allowing the next method to be called on the same object seamlessly (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

2. **Consistency and Simplicity**: By having methods return "self" by default, Smalltalk maintains a consistent and simple model where every method call results in an object being returned. This uniformity simplifies the mental model for developers, as they can always expect an object to be returned, even if it's just the receiver itself (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

3. **Encapsulation and Message Passing**: Smalltalk's design emphasizes message passing and encapsulation. Since instance variables are private and can only be accessed through messages, returning "self" by default supports this encapsulation by ensuring that methods can be chained without exposing internal state directly (Pharo By Example, p. 101).

### Example
Consider the following method:
```smalltalk
MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.
```
When you evaluate:
```smalltalk
| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.
```
The result of `myInstance myMethod` is `myInstance` itself, allowing further messages to be sent to `myInstance` in a chained manner (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

In summary, methods in Smalltalk return "self" by default to support method chaining, maintain consistency, and adhere to the principle of returning values only when they are of interest. This design choice enhances the readability, simplicity, and encapsulation of Smalltalk code.

supported_facts_labels: ['Kent Beck refers to this as the " Interesting return value " principle : " Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value " ( Pharo By Example , p. 104 ) .', 'Return a value only when you intend for the sender to use the value " ( Pharo By Example , p. 104 )"']

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if a number already exists in a `Set` in Pharo and ensure it is not added again, you can use the `includes:` method. The `Set` class in Pharo is designed to behave as a mathematical set, meaning it does not allow duplicate elements (Pharo By Example, pp. 207-208). Here is how you can modify your function to check for the existence of an element before adding it:

```smalltalk
Myfunction
| list length data z x |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := "received data after each refresh".
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [:k | 
    list := data at: k.
    (list < x and: [z includes: list not])
        ifTrue: [z add: list]
].
```

In this code:
1. `z := Set new.` initializes a new empty set.
2. `data` is assumed to be the collection of numbers received after each refresh.
3. The loop iterates over each element in `data`.
4. For each element (`list`), it checks if the element is less than `x` and not already included in the set `z` using `(list < x and: [z includes: list not])`.
5. If both conditions are true, the element is added to the set `z` using `z add: list`.

This ensures that only unique elements that meet the condition `list < x` are added to the set. The `includes:` method is used to check for membership in the set (Pharo By Example, p. 208).
supporting_facts_labels: ['Set The class Set is a collection which behaves as a mathematical set , i.e. , as a collection with no duplicate elements and without any order .']
supported_facts_labels: ['The ` Set ` class in Pharo is designed to behave as a mathematical set , meaning it does not allow duplicate elements ( Pharo By Example , pp . 207 - 208 ) .']

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use a series of conditional statements (`ifTrue:ifFalse:`) to handle different cases. Pharo does not have a built-in switch statement, but you can achieve similar functionality using these conditionals.

Here is a refined version of your code with explanations and improvements:

```smalltalk
"Check if the program ends with a newline"
endsWithNewLine := aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue: [
    "Split commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do: [:com |
        "Split each command into command and value"
        parts := com splitOn: ' '.
        command := parts at: 1.
        value := (parts at: 2) asInteger.
        
        "Execute the corresponding turtle command"
        command = 'down'
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: value ]
            ifFalse: [
                command = 'left'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle left: value ]
                    ifFalse: [
                        command = 'right'
                            ifTrue: [ turtle right: value ]
                            ifFalse: [
                                command = 'up'
                                    ifTrue: [ turtle up: value ]
                                    ifFalse: [ self error: 'Unknown command' ] ] ] ] ].
```

### Explanation:

1. **Splitting Commands**:
   - The program first checks if the input ends with a newline (`String cr`).
   - If true, it splits the input into individual commands using `splitOn: String cr` (pp. 66).

2. **Processing Each Command**:
   - Each command is further split into the command part and the value part using `splitOn: ' '` (pp. 66).
   - The command part is extracted using `parts at: 1` and the value part is converted to an integer using `(parts at: 2) asInteger`.

3. **Conditional Execution**:
   - The code uses a series of `ifTrue:ifFalse:` statements to check the command and execute the corresponding method on the `turtle` object (pp. 66).
   - If the command is not recognized, it raises an error using `self error: 'Unknown command'`.

### Debugging in Pharo:

To open the debugger in Pharo, you can manually trigger an error or use the `self halt` statement to pause execution and inspect the state of your program (pp. 51). For example, you can insert `self halt` at any point in your code to open the debugger:

```smalltalk
self halt.
```

This will allow you to step through your code and inspect variables, helping you understand what is happening at each step.

By following these steps, you can effectively implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk and debug your code when necessary.

supported_facts_labels: ['For example , you can insert ` self halt ` at any point in your code to open the debugger :    ` ` ` smalltalk   self halt .']

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you need to use a `FileStream` and set the appropriate text converter for the desired encoding. The `FileStream` class provides methods to handle different encodings by setting a converter. Here is how you can do it:

1. **Open the FileStream**: Use `FileStream readOnlyFileNamed:` to open the file for reading.
2. **Set the Converter**: Use the `converter:` message to set the appropriate text converter for the desired encoding.

For example, if you want to read a file encoded in Latin1, you can use the `Latin1TextConverter`:

```smalltalk
| stream line |
stream := FileStream readOnlyFileNamed: 'test.txt'.
stream converter: Latin1TextConverter new.
line := stream nextLine.
stream close.
```

This code snippet opens the file `test.txt`, sets the converter to `Latin1TextConverter`, reads the next line, and then closes the stream. This approach ensures that the file is read with the correct encoding.

Here is a more detailed example that demonstrates reading a file with a specific encoding:

```smalltalk
| stream line |
stream := FileStream readOnlyFileNamed: 'test.txt'.
stream converter: Latin1TextConverter new.
line := stream nextLine.
Transcript show: line; cr.
stream close.
```

In this example, the content of the first line of the file is printed to the Transcript, ensuring it is read with the Latin1 encoding.

For other encodings, you can use the corresponding text converter, such as `UTF8TextConverter` for UTF-8 encoding, or any other available converter in Pharo.

References:
- "Pharo By Example", p. 232-233, 329.
