title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
To create a class in Pharo Smalltalk, you typically use the System Browser, which is the central development tool in Pharo. Here’s a step-by-step guide based on the information from "Pharo By Example":

1. **Open the System Browser**: Navigate to the System Browser from the World menu.

2. **Select a Package**: In the left-most pane, select the package where you want to create your new class. If you need to create a new package, right-click in the package pane and select "Add package..." (pp. 119-120).

3. **Use the Class Creation Template**: When you click on the selected package, the bottom browser pane will display a class creation template. This template is a Pharo expression that sends a message to an existing class (usually `Object`), asking it to create a subclass (pp. 119-120). For example:
   ```smalltalk
   Object subclass: #Dog
       instanceVariableNames: 'count'
       classVariableNames: ''
       package: 'TestC'
   ```

4. **Edit the Template**: Replace `Object` with the superclass you want to inherit from (usually `Object` or one of its subclasses), replace `#Dog` with the name of your new class, and fill in the instance variable names if you know them (pp. 119-120).

5. **Accept the Changes**: Either right-click and select "Accept" or use the shortcut CMD-s (for "Save"). This will compile the new class definition (p. 36).

6. **Define Class and Instance Methods**: Once the class is created, you can define methods for it. Methods on the instance side are those that will be available to instances of the class, while methods on the class side are available to the class itself (pp. 91-92). For example, to define a class method to initialize a class variable:
   ```smalltalk
   Dog class >> initialize
       count := 0.
   ```

   And to define an instance method:
   ```smalltalk
   Dog >> bark
       Transcript show: 'Woof!'; cr.
   ```

7. **Class and Instance Variables**: Instance variables are private to the instance itself and can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them, and also in the methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). Class instance variables are private to the class and are not shared with subclasses (p. 92).

8. **Testing the Class**: You can test your class in a Playground or Transcript. For example:
   ```smalltalk
   Dog initialize.
   aDog := Dog new.
   aDog bark.  "Outputs: Woof!"
   Dog count.  "Outputs: 1"
   ```

In summary, creating a class in Pharo involves using the System Browser to edit a class creation template, defining instance and class methods, and understanding the encapsulation of instance and class variables. This process allows you to leverage Pharo's powerful live coding environment to develop and test your classes interactively (pp. 1-2, 91-92, 119-120).

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
In Pharo, you can dynamically generate getter and setter methods (accessors) for a class using a combination of reflection and meta-programming techniques. Here’s a detailed explanation of how you can achieve this:

### Dynamic Generation of Accessors

Pharo allows you to dynamically add methods to a class, including getter and setter methods for instance variables. This can be particularly useful when you have a long list of instance variables, such as those derived from an existing SQL database.

#### Step-by-Step Process

1. **Define the Class and Instance Variables:**
   You can define a new class and its instance variables using the class creation template in the System Browser. For example:
   ```smalltalk
   Object subclass: #MyClass
       instanceVariableNames: 'aaaa bbbb cccc'
       classVariableNames: ''
       poolDictionaries: ''
       category: 'MyCategory'.
   ```
   (pp. 119-120)

2. **Generate Accessors Dynamically:**
   You can use the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to dynamically generate accessors when they are first called. Here’s an example of how you can implement this:
   ```smalltalk
   DynamicAccessors >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
       | messageName |
       messageName := aMessage selector asString.
       (self class instVarNames includes: messageName)
           ifTrue: [
               self class compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
               ^ aMessage sendTo: self ].
       ^ super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.
   ```
   This method traps any message not understood by the object. If the message corresponds to an instance variable, it dynamically compiles a getter method for that variable and then resends the message to itself (p. 315).

3. **Automate the Process for Multiple Variables:**
   To automate the creation of accessors for a list of instance variables, you can define a method that takes a class and a list of variable names, and generates the corresponding getter and setter methods:
   ```smalltalk
   addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: #(aaaa bbbb cccc)
       variablesList do: [:varName |
           MyClass compile: varName, String cr, ' ^ ', varName.
           MyClass compile: varName, ': anObject', String cr, varName, ' := anObject.'].
   ```
   This method iterates over the list of variable names, compiling both getter and setter methods for each variable (p. 315).

### Example Usage

Here’s how you can use the above method to add variables and accessors to a class:
```smalltalk
addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: #(aaaa bbbb cccc).
```
This will result in the creation of getter and setter methods for the instance variables `aaaa`, `bbbb`, and `cccc` in the class `MyClass`.

### Best Practices

- **Encapsulation:** In Pharo, instance variables are private to the object that owns them. Access to these variables should be provided through accessor methods (p. 49, p. 89).
- **Readability:** Pharo emphasizes code readability. Ensure that dynamically generated methods are well-documented and follow naming conventions (p. 36).

By following these steps, you can efficiently generate getter and setter methods for a class in Pharo, making your code more maintainable and adhering to best practices in object-oriented programming.

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something every 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you need to ensure that the delay and printing operations are executed sequentially rather than concurrently. The issue with your initial code is that it forks a new process for each iteration, causing all the delays to run in parallel and thus printing all messages at once after 2 seconds.

Here is a corrected version of your code that ensures the delay and printing operations are executed sequentially:

```smalltalk
| i delay |
i := 0.
delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    delay wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'; cr.
    i := i + 1.
].
```

In this version, the `delay wait` and `Transcript show:` operations are executed in the same process, ensuring that each message is printed after a 2-second delay. The `whileTrue:` loop will iterate 5 times, printing "2 seconds are up" every 2 seconds.

### Explanation:
1. **Initialization**: 
   - `i := 0` initializes the counter.
   - `delay := Delay forSeconds: 2` creates a delay object that represents a 2-second delay.

2. **Loop**:
   - `[i < 5] whileTrue:` starts a loop that will run as long as `i` is less than 5.
   - `delay wait` pauses the execution for 2 seconds.
   - `Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'; cr` prints the message to the Transcript and moves to a new line.
   - `i := i + 1` increments the counter.

This approach ensures that the delay and the printing are done sequentially within the same loop iteration, achieving the desired effect of printing the message every 2 seconds.

### References:
- The use of `Delay` and `wait` for pausing execution is discussed in the context of managing execution timing in Pharo (Pharo By Example, pp. 13-14).
- The `Transcript` is used for outputting text, and methods like `show:` and `cr` are used to print messages and add carriage returns (Pharo By Example, pp. 17-18).

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk, and by extension Pharo, is referred to as a "dynamically typed" language because it does not require explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, or method returns. Instead, types are associated with objects at runtime rather than at compile time. This means that the type of an object is determined dynamically when the program is executed, rather than being checked statically by the compiler before execution (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100).

In Pharo, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects. This dynamic message sending is a core aspect of the language. When a message is sent to an object, the object itself determines how to respond to the message, which is a dynamic operation. This contrasts with statically typed languages where the method to be executed is determined at compile time (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).

For example, consider the following Pharo code:
```smalltalk
3 + 4
>>> 7
(1@2) + 4
>>> 5@6
```
In this code, the message `+` is sent to different objects (an integer and a point), and each object has its own method for responding to the `+` message. The method to be executed is chosen dynamically at runtime based on the receiver of the message (Pharo By Example, p. 100).

The term "dynamically typed" is used instead of "untyped" because, although there are no explicit type declarations, types still exist and are checked at runtime. The class of an object can be queried using messages like `class` or `isMemberOf:`. For instance:
```smalltalk
1 class
>>> SmallInteger
1 isMemberOf: SmallInteger
>>> true
```
This shows that objects do have types (their classes), and these types can be inspected dynamically (Pharo By Example, pp. 183-184).

In summary, Smalltalk (and Pharo) is called "dynamically typed" because type information is associated with objects at runtime, and type checking occurs dynamically as the program executes. This dynamic nature allows for a flexible and interactive programming environment, which is a hallmark of the Smalltalk family of languages (Pharo By Example, pp. 1, 100-101).

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, "instanceVariableNames" and "classVariableNames" serve distinct purposes and are used to define different types of variables within a class.

**Instance Variables:**
Instance variables are defined using the "instanceVariableNames" keyword. These variables are private to each instance of the class, meaning that each object created from the class has its own separate copy of these variables. They can be accessed by name within any instance method of the class that defines them, as well as in methods defined in its subclasses (p. 89). However, it is considered bad style in Pharo to access an instance variable directly from a subclass (p. 89). This encapsulation ensures that the internal state of an object is protected and can only be accessed or modified through methods defined in the class.

For example:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'
```
In this example, each instance of `MyClass` will have its own `name` and `age` variables.

**Class Variables:**
Class variables, defined using the "classVariableNames" keyword, are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. This means that there is only one copy of a class variable, and it is accessible from any instance of the class as well as from the class itself (pp. 109-110). Class variables are useful when you need to maintain a state or data that should be consistent across all instances of a class.

For example:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: 'SharedCounter'
    category: 'MyPackage'
```
In this example, `SharedCounter` is a class variable that is shared among all instances of `MyClass`.

**Class Instance Variables:**
Class instance variables are similar to instance variables but are defined for the class itself, which is an instance of its metaclass. These variables are private to the class and its subclasses, meaning that each class has its own set of class instance variables, and subclasses inherit these variables but have their own private copies (p. 92). This is different from class variables, which are shared among all instances and the class itself.

For example:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'
MyClass class
    instanceVariableNames: 'classInstanceVar'
```
In this example, `classInstanceVar` is a class instance variable that is private to `MyClass` and its subclasses.

In summary, instance variables are private to each instance of a class, class variables are shared among all instances and the class itself, and class instance variables are private to the class and its subclasses. Understanding these distinctions is crucial for managing state and behavior in Pharo effectively.

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return "self" by default if no explicit return value is specified. This design choice is rooted in the language's philosophy and has several practical benefits.

1. **Uniformity and Simplicity**: In Pharo, a dialect of Smalltalk, everything is an object, and objects communicate by sending messages. The pseudo-variable `self` represents the receiver of the message, similar to `this` in Java (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This uniformity simplifies the object model, making it easier to understand and predict the behavior of methods.

2. **Implicit Return of Self**: If a method does not explicitly return a value using the `^` construct, it implicitly returns the receiver (`self`). This can be imagined as if every method without an explicit return ends with `^ self` (Pharo By Example, p. 103). This implicit return ensures that the method always provides a value, maintaining consistency in message passing.

3. **Method Chaining**: One of the practical benefits of returning `self` by default is that it facilitates method chaining. Method chaining allows multiple methods to be called on the same object in a single statement, enhancing code readability and fluency. For example:
   ```smalltalk
   Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.
   ```
   Here, `show:` and `cr` are chained together because `Transcript` returns itself after each method call (Pharo By Example, p. 62).

4. **Communication of Intent**: Explicitly returning `self` can sometimes be avoided to communicate that the return value is not of particular interest to the sender. When you explicitly return `self`, you are indicating that the receiver itself is the significant result of the method (Pharo By Example, p. 103).

5. **Dynamic Method Lookup**: Methods in Pharo are dynamically dispatched, meaning that the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver and follows the inheritance chain. This dynamic nature ensures that the correct method is executed based on the actual class of the receiver at runtime (Pharo By Example, pp. 100-101).

In summary, methods return `self` by default in Smalltalk to maintain a simple and uniform object model, facilitate method chaining, and ensure consistent message passing. This design choice aligns with the language's philosophy of everything being an object and all interactions happening through message sends.

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if a number already exists in a set in Pharo and avoid adding it again, you can use the `includes:` method provided by the `Set` class. The `Set` class in Pharo is designed to hold unique elements, meaning it automatically handles duplicates by not adding an element if it already exists in the set. However, explicitly checking for membership before adding can be done using the `includes:` method.

Here is how you can modify your function to check if a number already exists in the set before adding it:

```smalltalk
Myfunction
| list length data z x |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := receivedDataAfterEachRefresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [:k | 
    list := data at: k.
    (list < x and: [z includes: list not]) ifTrue: [z add: list].
].
```

In this code:
- `z := Set new.` initializes a new empty set.
- `data := receivedDataAfterEachRefresh.` assumes `data` is the collection of numbers received after each refresh.
- The loop `1 to: length do: [:k | ... ]` iterates over each element in `data`.
- `list := data at: k.` retrieves the k-th element from `data`.
- The condition `(list < x and: [z includes: list not])` checks if the element is less than `x` and not already included in the set `z`.
- If the condition is true, `z add: list` adds the element to the set.

This approach ensures that only unique elements less than `x` are added to the set `z`.

References:
- "The class Set is a collection which behaves as a mathematical set, i.e., as a collection with no duplicate elements and without any order" (Pharo By Example, pp. 207-208).
- "The set operations union, intersection and membership test are implemented by the Collection messages union:, intersection:, and includes:" (Pharo By Example, p. 208).
- "Testing set membership: MyExampleSetTest >> testIncludes self assert: (full includes: 5)" (Pharo By Example, p. 163).

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use a series of conditional expressions. Pharo does not have a dedicated switch statement, but you can achieve similar functionality using nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages sent to Boolean objects. Here’s how you can refactor your code to make it more readable and maintainable:

1. **Refactor the Command Parsing**: Split the command and its value, then use nested conditionals to handle different commands.

2. **Use the Debugger**: If you need to debug your code, you can insert a `self halt` statement to open the debugger at a specific point in your code.

Here is a refactored version of your code:

```smalltalk
| endsWithNewLine commands |
endsWithNewLine := aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue: [
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do: [:com |
        | parts command value |
        parts := com splitOn: ' '.
        command := parts first.
        value := parts second asInteger.
        
        command = 'down'
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: value ]
            ifFalse: [
                command = 'left'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle left: value ]
                    ifFalse: [
                        command = 'right'
                            ifTrue: [ turtle right: value ]
                            ifFalse: [
                                command = 'up'
                                    ifTrue: [ turtle up: value ]
                                    ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
```

### Explanation:

1. **Splitting Commands**: The program first checks if the input ends with a newline and then splits the commands based on new lines.
2. **Parsing Each Command**: Each command is split into the command part and the value part.
3. **Conditional Execution**: The command is compared against possible values ('down', 'left', 'right', 'up') using nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages. If none of the conditions match, an assertion failure is triggered.

### Debugging:

To open the debugger at a specific point, you can insert `self halt` in your code. For example:

```smalltalk
command = 'down'
    ifTrue: [ self halt. turtle down: value ]
    ifFalse: [
        command = 'left'
            ifTrue: [ turtle left: value ]
            ifFalse: [
                command = 'right'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle right: value ]
                    ifFalse: [
                        command = 'up'
                            ifTrue: [ turtle up: value ]
                            ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ].
```

When the code execution reaches `self halt`, the debugger will open, allowing you to inspect the state of your program.

### References:

- Pharo does not have special syntax for control constructs; instead, these are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections with blocks as arguments (Pharo By Example, p. 66).
- Conditionals are expressed by sending messages like `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to the result of a boolean expression (Pharo By Example, p. 66).
- The debugger can be invoked by inserting a `Halt now` expression in the code (Pharo By Example, p. 51).

By following these guidelines, you can effectively implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk and utilize the debugger to troubleshoot your code.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you need to use a text converter that matches the file's encoding. The `FileStream` class provides a way to specify the encoding by setting a converter. Here is how you can do it:

1. **Open the file stream**: Use `FileStream` to open the file.
2. **Set the converter**: Assign the appropriate text converter to the stream.
3. **Read the file**: Use the stream to read the file content.

Here is an example of how to read a file with a specific encoding, such as Latin1:

```smalltalk
| dir stream line |
dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt') readStream.
stream converter: Latin1TextConverter new.
line := stream nextLine.
stream close.
```

In this example:
- `FileSystem disk workingDirectory` gets the current working directory.
- `(dir / 'test.txt') readStream` opens a read stream for the file `test.txt`.
- `stream converter: Latin1TextConverter new` sets the text converter to `Latin1TextConverter` to handle Latin1 encoding.
- `line := stream nextLine` reads the next line from the file.
- `stream close` closes the stream after reading.

This approach ensures that the file is read correctly according to its encoding. If you need to use a different encoding, replace `Latin1TextConverter` with the appropriate converter for that encoding.

For more details, refer to the following sections in the textbook "Pharo By Example":
- "File streams are by default character-based. Send binary to explicitly make them binary." (p. 234)
- "WebPage >> contents ^ (FileStream oldFileOrNoneNamed: path) converter: Latin1TextConverter new; contents" (p. 329)

These sections explain how to handle file streams and set text converters for different encodings in Pharo.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
In Pharo, when a message is sent to an object, the system first looks for a corresponding method in the object's class. If the method is not found, the lookup continues up the class hierarchy until it reaches the `Object` class. If no method is found by then, the virtual machine causes the object to send itself the message `doesNotUnderstand:` with the original message selector as its argument (Pharo By Example, pp. 101, 107).

The `doesNotUnderstand:` method is implemented in the `Object` class. When invoked, it creates a `MessageNotUnderstood` object and typically starts a debugger in the current execution context (Pharo By Example, pp. 107-108). This mechanism allows developers to intercept such errors and take alternative actions by overriding `doesNotUnderstand:` in any subclass of `Object` (Pharo By Example, pp. 107, 312-313).

In your case, the message `selectors` is understood by the `receiver`, returning `#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)`. However, when you send the message `expression` to the same `receiver`, it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This indicates that the `expression` method is not implemented in the `receiver`'s class or any of its superclasses.

To fix this issue, you need to ensure that the `expression` method is defined in the class of the `receiver` or one of its superclasses. If the method should exist but is missing, you might need to implement it or check if it is being dynamically loaded or compiled, as sometimes `doesNotUnderstand:` is used to lazily load or compile missing methods (Pharo By Example, p. 322).

Here is an example of how you might define the `expression` method in the `receiver`'s class:

```smalltalk
ReceiverClass >> expression
    "Implement the method to handle the expression message"
    ^ "Your implementation here"
```

By ensuring the method is defined, the `MessageNotUnderstood` error will be resolved, and the `receiver` will correctly respond to the `expression` message.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can use the `OSProcess` package, which provides functionality similar to the `system()` function in Unix-like languages. This package allows you to run external shell commands and capture their output.

Here is a concise guide on how to do this:

1. **Install the OSProcess Package**:
   First, you need to ensure that the `OSProcess` package is installed in your Pharo environment. You can do this by using the Monticello Browser to load the package from a repository.

2. **Using OSProcess to Run Shell Commands**:
   Once the package is installed, you can use it to run shell commands. Here is an example of how to execute a shell command and capture its output:

   ```smalltalk
   | result |
   result := OSProcess command: 'ls -l'.
   Transcript show: result output; cr.
   ```

   In this example, the `OSProcess command:` method runs the `ls -l` command (which lists directory contents in Unix-like systems) and captures its output. The result is then printed to the Transcript.

3. **Capturing Command Output**:
   The `OSProcess` package provides methods to capture the standard output and standard error of the executed command. You can access these using the `output` and `error` methods on the result object.

   ```smalltalk
   | result |
   result := OSProcess command: 'ls -l'.
   Transcript show: 'Output: ', result output; cr.
   Transcript show: 'Error: ', result error; cr.
   ```

   This will display both the standard output and any error messages generated by the command.

4. **Running Commands Asynchronously**:
   If you need to run commands asynchronously, `OSProcess` also supports this. You can start a process and continue with other tasks while it runs.

   ```smalltalk
   | process |
   process := OSProcess command: 'sleep 10'.
   "Do other tasks here"
   process waitForExit.
   Transcript show: 'Process finished'; cr.
   ```

   In this example, the `sleep 10` command runs for 10 seconds, and the script waits for its completion using `waitForExit`.

By using the `OSProcess` package, you can effectively run and manage external shell commands within the Pharo environment, similar to how you would use `system()` or backticks in Unix-like languages (Pharo By Example, pp. 13-14).

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
To set the day, month, and year values for a `Date` or `DateAndTime` object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak), you can use the class methods provided by these classes. Specifically, you can use `Date class>>fromDays:`, `Date class>>year:month:day:`, and `DateAndTime class>>year:month:day:hour:minute:second:offset:`.

For creating a `Date` object, you can use the `year:month:day:` method. This method allows you to create a new `Date` by supplying three integers: one for the year, one for the month, and one for the day of the month. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| aDate |
aDate := Date year: 2023 month: 10 day: 5.
aDate.  "This will return a Date object representing October 5, 2023"
```
This method is straightforward and directly sets the year, month, and day values for the `Date` object (Pharo By Example, p. 132).

For creating a `DateAndTime` object, you can use the `year:month:day:hour:minute:second:offset:` method. This method allows you to create a new `DateAndTime` by supplying integers for the year, month, day, hour, minute, second, and the time zone offset. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| aDateTime |
aDateTime := DateAndTime year: 2023 month: 10 day: 5 hour: 14 minute: 30 second: 0 offset: 0.
aDateTime.  "This will return a DateAndTime object representing October 5, 2023, 14:30:00 UTC"
```
This method is useful when you need to specify not only the date but also the time and time zone offset (Pharo By Example, p. 132).

These methods provide a convenient way to set all three parameters (day, month, and year) at once, ensuring that the `Date` or `DateAndTime` objects are correctly initialized with the specified values.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a `Dictionary` by its values in Smalltalk and obtain an `OrderedCollection` of the keys in descending order, you can follow these steps:

1. **Extract the associations from the dictionary**: Use the `associationsDo:` message to iterate over the dictionary's associations (key-value pairs).

2. **Sort the associations by their values**: Convert the associations to a collection and sort it using a custom sort block that compares the values.

3. **Collect the keys from the sorted associations**: Extract the keys from the sorted associations to form the final `OrderedCollection`.

Here is a step-by-step example based on the provided dictionary:

```smalltalk
| dictionary sortedAssociations sortedKeys |
dictionary := Dictionary newFrom: { #Rankable1 -> 8.5. #Rankable2 -> 9.0 }.

"Step 1: Extract associations and sort them by values in descending order"
sortedAssociations := (dictionary associations asSortedCollection: [:a :b | a value > b value]).

"Step 2: Collect the keys from the sorted associations"
sortedKeys := sortedAssociations collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

"sortedKeys now contains the keys in the desired order"
sortedKeys
```

### Explanation:

1. **Extract Associations**:
   ```smalltalk
   dictionary associations
   ```
   This returns a collection of associations (key-value pairs) from the dictionary.

2. **Sort Associations**:
   ```smalltalk
   asSortedCollection: [:a :b | a value > b value]
   ```
   This sorts the associations by their values in descending order. The block `[:a :b | a value > b value]` specifies the sorting criterion.

3. **Collect Keys**:
   ```smalltalk
   collect: [:assoc | assoc key]
   ```
   This collects the keys from the sorted associations into a new collection.

### Result:
The `sortedKeys` variable will contain an `OrderedCollection` with the keys sorted by their corresponding values in descending order:
```smalltalk
#(Rankable2 Rankable1)
```

### References:
- Iterating over dictionary associations: "The proper messages to use are `keysDo:`, `valuesDo:`, and `associationsDo:`, which iterate respectively on keys, values or associations." (Pharo By Example, p. 214)
- Sorting collections: "To sort a collection, send it the message `asSortedCollection`." (Pharo By Example, p. 219)
- Custom sorting with a block: "You can do this by supplying a two argument block, called a sortblock, to the sorted collection." (Pharo By Example, p. 209)

"', '( Pharo By Example , p. 219 )   - Custom sorting with a block : " You can do this by supplying a two argument block , called a sortblock , to the sorted collection . "', "Use the ` associationsDo : ` message to iterate over the dictionary 's associations ( key - value pairs ) ."]

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements in Pharo, you can use the `asSet` method to convert both arrays into sets and then compare these sets for equality. This approach works because sets inherently handle duplicate elements and do not consider the order of elements, which aligns with the requirement of checking for the same elements regardless of order or duplicates.

Here is a concise way to achieve this:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
```

This code converts both `arr1` and `arr2` to sets and then checks if these sets are equal. If they are equal, it returns `true`; otherwise, it returns `false`.

### Explanation:

1. **Conversion to Sets**:
   - `asSet` converts an array to a set, which automatically handles duplicates and ignores the order of elements (Pharo By Example, pp. 200-201).

2. **Equality Check**:
   - The `=` operator in Pharo checks for object equality, meaning it will return `true` if the sets contain the same elements (Pharo By Example, p. 182).

### Example:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(6 5 3 1).

(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
```

In this example, `arr1` and `arr2` contain the same elements but in different orders. Converting them to sets and comparing will return `true` because sets do not consider the order of elements.

### Important Notes:

- **Object Equality vs. Identity**:
  - In Pharo, `=` tests for object equality (whether two objects represent the same value), while `==` tests for object identity (whether two expressions represent the same object) (Pharo By Example, p. 182).
  
- **Handling Duplicates**:
  - Sets inherently handle duplicates, so if the arrays contain duplicate elements, they will be considered equal as long as the sets of unique elements are the same (Pharo By Example, p. 200).

By using sets, you simplify the problem of checking for the same elements in two arrays, leveraging the properties of sets to handle duplicates and order automatically.

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
Choosing between Squeak and Pharo for a beginning Smalltalker, especially for someone with a background in Ruby, involves understanding the strengths and weaknesses of each environment. Both Squeak and Pharo are implementations of the Smalltalk programming language, but they have different focuses and communities.

### Pharo

**Pros:**
1. **Modern and Actively Developed**: Pharo is designed to be a modern, clean, and innovative Smalltalk environment. It does not just replicate the past but reinvents the essence of Smalltalk with evolutionary and incremental changes【4:2†source】【4:9†source】.
2. **Live Coding Environment**: Pharo offers a live coding environment that allows you to modify code while it executes, which can significantly enhance productivity and experimentation【4:8†source】.
3. **Powerful IDE**: Pharo provides a powerful integrated development environment (IDE) with tools to manage complex code and promote good design practices【4:8†source】.
4. **Rich Library and Virtual OS**: Pharo includes a rich library and can be viewed as a virtual operating system, with a fast JITing VM and full access to OS libraries via its Foreign Function Interface (FFI)【4:8†source】.
5. **Community and Culture**: Pharo has a welcoming community that encourages contributions and values improvements. This culture can be very supportive for beginners【4:8†source】.
6. **Portability**: Pharo is highly portable and can run on various platforms including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi【4:9†source】.

**Cons:**
1. **Learning Curve**: While Pharo is powerful, its modern features and extensive libraries might present a steeper learning curve for absolute beginners【4:6†source】.
2. **Frequent Updates**: The active development and frequent updates might sometimes lead to instability or require frequent learning of new features【4:2†source】.

### Squeak

**Pros:**
1. **Educational Focus**: Squeak has a strong focus on educational use and multimedia applications. It is often used in academic settings and for teaching purposes, which might make it more approachable for beginners【4:9†source】.
2. **Rich History and Community**: Squeak has a long history and a large community, which means there is a wealth of resources, tutorials, and community support available【4:9†source】.
3. **Stable and Mature**: Squeak is known for its stability and maturity, making it a reliable choice for learning and experimentation【4:9†source】.

**Cons:**
1. **Less Modern**: Compared to Pharo, Squeak might feel less modern and innovative. It tends to stick closer to traditional Smalltalk principles without as many of the modern enhancements found in Pharo【4:9†source】.
2. **User Interface**: The user interface of Squeak might feel outdated compared to the more polished and modern interface of Pharo【4:9†source】.

### Conclusion

For a Rubyist starting with Smalltalk, **Pharo** might be the better choice if you are looking for a modern, actively developed environment with a powerful IDE and a supportive community. Its live coding environment and rich libraries can provide a robust platform for learning and experimentation.

On the other hand, if you prefer a more stable and educationally focused environment with a rich history and extensive community resources, **Squeak** could be the way to go. Its stability and maturity make it a reliable choice for beginners.

Ultimately, the choice depends on your specific needs and preferences. If you value modern features and active development, go with Pharo. If you prefer stability and a focus on education, Squeak might be more suitable.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, it is indeed possible to add a string to a `String` instance via a method. This can be achieved using several approaches, such as concatenation with the comma operator or using a `WriteStream`.

### Using the Comma Operator
The comma operator (`,`) is used to concatenate two strings. This operator is defined by the `Collection` class, so it works for any kind of collection, including strings. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
renderThisOn: aString
    aString := aString, 'whatever text I want'.
```

In this example, `aString` is concatenated with `'whatever text I want'` using the comma operator, and the result is assigned back to `aString`【4:2†source】【4:7†source】.

### Using WriteStream
Another efficient way to concatenate strings is by using a `WriteStream`. This method is particularly useful when you need to perform multiple concatenations, as it avoids the creation of intermediate strings:

```smalltalk
renderThisOn: aString
    | stream |
    stream := WriteStream on: aString.
    stream nextPutAll: 'whatever text I want'.
    ^ stream contents.
```

In this example, a `WriteStream` is created on `aString`, and the `nextPutAll:` method is used to append `'whatever text I want'` to the stream. Finally, `stream contents` returns the concatenated string【4:1†source】.

### Example in Context
If you want a `String` instance to behave like the "html" object in Seaside, where you pass it as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it, you can use either of the above methods. Here is a more comprehensive example:

```smalltalk
renderThisOn: aString
    self addHeaderTo: aString.
    self addBodyTo: aString.
    self addFooterTo: aString.

addHeaderTo: aString
    aString := aString, '<header>Header Content</header>'.

addBodyTo: aString
    aString := aString, '<body>Body Content</body>'.

addFooterTo: aString
    aString := aString, '<footer>Footer Content</footer>'.
```

In this example, `aString` is passed to multiple methods (`addHeaderTo:`, `addBodyTo:`, and `addFooterTo:`), each of which appends specific content to the string using the comma operator【4:2†source】【4:7†source】.

By using these methods, you can effectively add strings to a `String` instance in Smalltalk, allowing it to accumulate content as it is passed through various methods.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can easily check if an object is an instance of a given class or any of its subclasses using the `isKindOf:` method. This method is built into the language and provides a straightforward way to perform this check.

The `isKindOf:` method is defined on the `Object` class and answers whether the receiver’s class is either the same as, or a subclass of, the argument class. Here is how you can use it:

```smalltalk
anObject isKindOf: SomeClass
```

This will return `true` if `anObject` is an instance of `SomeClass` or any subclass of `SomeClass`, and `false` otherwise.

### Examples

1. Checking if a number is a kind of `SmallInteger`:
    ```smalltalk
    1 isKindOf: SmallInteger
    ```
    This will return `true` because `1` is an instance of `SmallInteger`.

2. Checking if a number is a kind of `Integer`:
    ```smalltalk
    1 isKindOf: Integer
    ```
    This will also return `true` because `SmallInteger` is a subclass of `Integer`.

3. Checking if a number is a kind of `Number`:
    ```smalltalk
    1 isKindOf: Number
    ```
    This will return `true` because `Integer` (and thus `SmallInteger`) is a subclass of `Number`.

4. Checking if a number is a kind of `Object`:
    ```smalltalk
    1 isKindOf: Object
    ```
    This will return `true` because all classes in Smalltalk ultimately inherit from `Object`.

5. Checking if a number is a kind of `String`:
    ```smalltalk
    1 isKindOf: String
    ```
    This will return `false` because `SmallInteger` is not a subclass of `String`.

These examples illustrate how `isKindOf:` can be used to determine class membership and inheritance relationships in Smalltalk【4:2†source】【4:9†source】.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
To print an integer to the Transcript in Pharo, you need to ensure that you are using the correct methods and syntax. The code you provided has a few issues that need to be addressed.

First, the method `SmallInteger new: 0` is incorrect because `SmallInteger` does not have a `new:` method. Instead, you should directly assign an integer value to the variable. Here is the corrected version of your code:

```smalltalk
| myNum |
myNum := 0.
Transcript show: myNum printString.
```

This code will correctly print the integer `0` to the Transcript. Let's break down the steps:

1. **Variable Assignment**: `myNum := 0.` assigns the integer `0` to the variable `myNum`. In Pharo, integers are directly assigned without needing to use a constructor like `new:`【4:7†source】.

2. **Printing to Transcript**: `Transcript show: myNum printString.` converts the integer to a string using `printString` and then displays it in the Transcript. The `Transcript` is a global object used for logging and debugging purposes【4:4†source】【4:3†source】.

The corrected code avoids the crash by properly assigning an integer value and using the correct method to display it in the Transcript. Here is an example of how to display a string in the Transcript, which is similar to displaying an integer:

```smalltalk
Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.
```

This example shows how to display the string `'hello'` and then move to a new line using `cr` (carriage return)【4:4†source】.

By following these steps, you can successfully print an integer to the Transcript in Pharo without causing a crash.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
To match all words in a phrase using regular expressions in Pharo Smalltalk, you need to adjust your regular expression and the way you apply it. The regular expression `[A-Za-z]*` only matches zero or more alphabetic characters, but it does not account for word boundaries or spaces between words, which is why it only returns the first word.

Here’s how you can modify your approach:

1. **Use a Regex to Match Words**: You should use a regular expression that matches word boundaries. In regex, `\b` represents a word boundary, and `\w+` matches one or more word characters (letters, digits, and underscores). Therefore, the regex `\b\w+\b` will match each word in a string.

2. **Iterate Over Matches**: In Pharo, you can use the `matchesIn:` method to find all matches of a regex in a string. This method returns an `OrderedCollection` of all matches.

Here is an example of how you can achieve this in Pharo:

```smalltalk
| phrase wordsRegex matches |
phrase := 'hello how are you'.
wordsRegex := '\b\w+\b' asRegex.
matches := wordsRegex matchesIn: phrase.
matches.  "This will return an OrderedCollection with 'hello', 'how', 'are', 'you'"
```

### Explanation:
- `phrase` is the string you want to search.
- `wordsRegex` is the regular expression that matches words.
- `matches := wordsRegex matchesIn: phrase` finds all matches of the regex in the string and stores them in `matches`.

### Example Output:
The `matches` variable will contain an `OrderedCollection` with the words `#('hello' 'how' 'are' 'you')`.

### Additional Notes:
- The `matchesIn:` method is part of the `RxMatcher` class in Pharo, which provides various methods for iterating over matches within strings【4:2†source】【4:6†source】.
- Regular expressions in Pharo are powerful and can be used for various string manipulation tasks, including matching, replacing, and extracting substrings【4:4†source】【4:5†source】.

By using the correct regular expression and the appropriate method to find all matches, you can effectively match all words in a phrase in Pharo Smalltalk.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, the terms `self` and `yourself` are used within method bodies but serve different purposes.

### `self`
`self` is a keyword that refers to the object within which the current method is executing. It is known as the "receiver" because it is the object that received the message causing the method to execute. This keyword is a pseudo-variable, meaning it cannot be assigned a new value【4:0†source】【4:2†source】. 

For example, consider the following method definition:
```smalltalk
Morph >> fullPrintOn: aStream
    aStream nextPutAll: self class name, ' new'
```
In this method, `self` refers to the instance of `Morph` that received the `fullPrintOn:` message【4:9†source】.

### `yourself`
`yourself` is a method that simply returns the receiver object. It is often used to ensure that the method returns the receiver, especially in cases where the last message sent in a method might return a different object. This is useful for maintaining a fluent interface or method chaining.

For example:
```smalltalk
anObject initialize; yourself
```
In this case, `yourself` ensures that the method returns `anObject` rather than the result of `initialize`.

### Key Differences
- **Purpose**: `self` is used to refer to the receiver within the method body, allowing the method to send messages to itself. `yourself`, on the other hand, is used to return the receiver object explicitly.
- **Usage Context**: `self` is essential for invoking other methods on the same object, ensuring that the correct method is called even in subclasses. `yourself` is typically used at the end of a method to ensure the receiver is returned, which is particularly useful in method chaining.

In summary, while `self` is a pseudo-variable that refers to the receiver of the message, `yourself` is a method that returns the receiver, ensuring the method returns the object itself rather than another result【4:0†source】【4:8†source】.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, object initialization and parameter passing are handled differently compared to languages like Java and C++. Instead of using constructors with parameters, Pharo uses a combination of the `new` and `initialize` methods to set up objects.

### Initialization in Pharo Smalltalk

1. **Creating and Initializing Objects**:
   - In Pharo, the `new` method is used to create a new instance of a class. This method is typically defined on the class side and is responsible for allocating memory for the new object.
   - The `initialize` method is then called to set up the initial state of the object. This method is defined on the instance side and can be customized to initialize instance variables.

   ```smalltalk
   MyClass >> initialize
       "Initialize the instance variables"
       super initialize.
       "custom initialization code"
   ```

   When you create a new object, you typically call `new`, which in turn calls `initialize`:

   ```smalltalk
   MyClass class >> new
       ^ super new initialize
   ```

   This ensures that every new instance of `MyClass` is properly initialized.

2. **Passing Parameters**:
   - Unlike Java or C++, Pharo does not support passing parameters directly to the `new` method. Instead, you can define custom class-side methods that create and initialize objects with specific parameters.

   ```smalltalk
   MyClass class >> newWithColor: aColor
       ^ self new color: aColor
   ```

   In this example, `newWithColor:` is a class-side method that creates a new instance and sets its color.

3. **Setting Properties**:
   - After creating an object, you can set its properties using setter methods. This approach is similar to the second code snippet you provided.

   ```smalltalk
   | aColor |
   aColor := Color new.
   aColor name: 'red'.
   ```

   This method is more flexible and aligns with the Smalltalk philosophy of sending messages to objects to change their state.

### Example

Here is a complete example demonstrating how to create and initialize an object with a parameter in Pharo:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: MyClass [
    MyClass class >> newWithColor: aColor [
        ^ self new color: aColor
    ]

    MyClass >> initialize [
        super initialize.
        color := Color black. "default color"
    ]

    MyClass >> color: aColor [
        color := aColor
    ]
]
```

To create a new instance with a specific color:

```smalltalk
| myObject |
myObject := MyClass newWithColor: (Color red).
```

This approach ensures that the object is properly initialized and its properties are set in a clear and maintainable way【4:1†source】【4:3†source】【4:9†source】.
