supported_facts_labels: ['Explicitly returning " self " communicates to the sender that the returned value is of particular interest and is intended to be used .', 'The pseudo - variable " self " represents the receiver of the message , similar to " this " in Java .']

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` method. This method checks whether the argument is contained in the collection and returns a boolean value. If the element is already in the set, `includes:` will return `true`; otherwise, it will return `false`【4:6†source】【4:8†source】.

Here is how you can modify your function to check if a number already exists in the set before adding it:

```smalltalk
Myfunction
| list length data z x |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := "received data after each refresh".
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [:k | 
    list := data at: k.
    (list < x and: [z includes: list not]) ifTrue: [z add: list].
].
```

In this code:
- `z includes: list not` checks if `list` is not already in the set `z`.
- If the condition is true, it adds `list` to the set `z`.

This ensures that only unique elements less than `x` are added to the set `z`【4:6†source】【4:8†source】.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use nested conditional statements (`ifTrue:ifFalse:`) since Pharo does not have a dedicated switch statement syntax. Here’s a detailed explanation and an improved version of your code:

### Explanation

Pharo Smalltalk uses message sends to handle control structures. For conditionals, you typically use `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` messages sent to boolean expressions. Each of these messages takes blocks as arguments, which are evaluated based on the boolean condition【4:4†source】【4:6†source】.

### Improved Code

Here’s how you can refactor your code to handle the commands more cleanly using nested `ifTrue:ifFalse:` statements:

```smalltalk
"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine := aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue: [
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do: [:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        parts := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        command := parts at: 1.
        "value for command"
        value := (parts at: 2) asInteger.
        
        "attempted switch using nested ifTrue:ifFalse:"
        command = 'down'
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: value ]
            ifFalse: [
                command = 'left'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle left: value ]
                    ifFalse: [
                        command = 'right'
                            ifTrue: [ turtle right: value ]
                            ifFalse: [
                                command = 'up'
                                    ifTrue: [ turtle up: value ]
                                    ifFalse: [ self error: 'Unknown command' ] ] ] ] ].
```

### Key Points

1. **Conditional Statements**: Pharo uses `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:` for conditionals. These methods are sent to boolean expressions and take blocks as arguments【4:4†source】【4:6†source】.
2. **Blocks**: Blocks are enclosed in square brackets and can contain temporary variables and expressions. They are not evaluated until they receive a value message【4:2†source】.
3. **Error Handling**: Instead of `self assert: false`, use `self error: 'Unknown command'` to raise an error with a descriptive message if the command is not recognized.

### Debugging

To open the debugger in Pharo, you can insert `self halt` at the point where you want to start debugging. This will open the debugger when the code execution reaches that point.

```smalltalk
self halt.
```

Place this line before the code you want to debug, and it will trigger the debugger, allowing you to inspect the state of your program.

By following these guidelines, you can effectively implement a switch-like structure in Pharo Smalltalk and handle different commands for your turtle program.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you need to use the `ZnCharacterReadStream` class, which allows you to specify the encoding of the file. Here is how you can modify your code to read a file with a specific encoding:

```smalltalk
| dir stream line |
dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt') readStream.
stream := ZnCharacterReadStream on: stream encoding: 'ISO-8859-1'.
line := stream nextLine.
```

In this example, the `ZnCharacterReadStream` is used to wrap the original stream and specify the encoding as 'ISO-8859-1'. You can replace 'ISO-8859-1' with any other encoding you need, such as 'UTF-16' or 'ASCII'.

### Explanation:
1. **FileSystem and Stream Initialization**:
   ```smalltalk
   dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
   stream := (dir / 'test.txt') readStream.
   ```
   This part of the code initializes the file system and opens a read stream for the file 'test.txt' in the working directory.

2. **Specifying the Encoding**:
   ```smalltalk
   stream := ZnCharacterReadStream on: stream encoding: 'ISO-8859-1'.
   ```
   Here, `ZnCharacterReadStream` is used to wrap the existing stream and specify the desired encoding. The `encoding:` parameter allows you to set the encoding to 'ISO-8859-1' or any other encoding you need.

3. **Reading a Line**:
   ```smalltalk
   line := stream nextLine.
   ```
   Finally, the `nextLine` message is sent to the stream to read a line from the file with the specified encoding.

This approach ensures that the file is read correctly according to the specified encoding, avoiding issues that may arise from encoding mismatches.

### References:
- The use of `ZnCharacterReadStream` for handling different encodings is a common practice in Pharo for reading files with specific encodings【6:0†source】.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
The issue you're encountering with the `MessageNotUnderstood` error in Pharo can be understood by examining how message sending and method lookup work in Pharo, as well as the specific behavior of the `doesNotUnderstand:` method.

### Message Sending and Method Lookup

When you send a message to an object in Pharo, the system looks for a corresponding method in the method dictionary of the object's class. If the method is not found, the lookup continues up the class hierarchy until it reaches the `Object` class. If the method is still not found, the `doesNotUnderstand:` method is invoked, which typically raises a `MessageNotUnderstood` exception【6:1†source】【6:8†source】.

### Understanding the Error

In your case, the message `selectors` sent to `receiver` returns a list of selectors, indicating that the method `selectors` exists and is correctly implemented. However, when you send the message `expression` to the same `receiver`, it throws a `MessageNotUnderstood` error. This suggests that the method `expression` is not implemented in the class of the `receiver` or any of its superclasses.

### Possible Causes and Fixes

1. **Method Not Implemented**: The most straightforward reason is that the `expression` method is not defined in the class of the `receiver`. You can verify this by checking the class definition and its superclasses to ensure that the method exists.

2. **Typographical Error**: Ensure that there is no typo in the method name. Pharo is case-sensitive, so `expression` and `Expression` would be considered different methods.

3. **Dynamic Method Handling**: If you expect `expression` to be handled dynamically, ensure that the class implements a mechanism to handle such dynamic messages. This can be done by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to provide custom behavior when a message is not understood【6:8†source】.

### Example

Here is an example of how you might handle a `MessageNotUnderstood` error by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: MyClass [
    MyClass>>doesNotUnderstand: aMessage [
        "Custom handling for messages not understood"
        aMessage selector = #expression ifTrue: [ ^'Handled dynamically' ].
        ^super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
    ]
]
```

In this example, if the `expression` message is sent to an instance of `MyClass` and the method is not found, the `doesNotUnderstand:` method will handle it and return `'Handled dynamically'` instead of raising an error.

### Conclusion

The `MessageNotUnderstood` error occurs because the `expression` method is not found in the `receiver`'s class hierarchy. To fix this, ensure that the method is implemented, check for typos, or handle the message dynamically by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method【6:1†source】【6:8†source】.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
In Squeak and Pharo, you can invoke shell commands using the `OSProcess` and `CommandShell` packages, which provide functionality similar to the `system()` function in Unix-like languages and the backticks for capturing command output.

### Using OSProcess

The `OSProcess` package allows you to execute external commands and interact with the operating system. Here is an example of how to use `OSProcess` to run a shell command:

```smalltalk
| result |
result := OSProcess command: 'ls -l'.
Transcript show: result output; cr.
```

In this example, the `command:` method runs the `ls -l` command, and the output is captured and displayed in the Transcript【4:0†source】.

### Using CommandShell

The `CommandShell` package provides a more interactive way to run shell commands. It can be used to start a shell and execute commands interactively. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| shell |
shell := CommandShell new.
shell execute: 'ls -l'.
shell output.
```

This code snippet creates a new `CommandShell` instance, executes the `ls -l` command, and then retrieves the output【4:0†source】.

### Capturing Command Output

Both `OSProcess` and `CommandShell` allow you to capture the output of the commands. For instance, using `OSProcess`, you can capture the output as follows:

```smalltalk
| process output |
process := OSProcess command: 'echo Hello, World!'.
output := process output.
Transcript show: output; cr.
```

This will run the `echo Hello, World!` command and display the output in the Transcript【4:0†source】.

### Summary

- **OSProcess**: Use `OSProcess command:` to run shell commands and capture their output.
- **CommandShell**: Use `CommandShell` for a more interactive shell experience.

These tools provide robust ways to interact with the operating system from within Squeak and Pharo, similar to the `system()` function and backticks in Unix-like environments【4:0†source】【4:0†source】.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Pharo (or Squeak), you can create a new `Date` or `DateAndTime` object by supplying three integers: one for the day of the month (1-31), one for the month (1-12), and a four-digit integer for the year. Both classes provide class methods to facilitate this.

### Creating a `Date` Object

To create a `Date` object, you can use the class method `Date class>>fromDays:`, which takes the number of days since the Smalltalk epoch (January 1, 1901). However, for a more straightforward approach, you can use the `Date class>>year:month:day:` method, which directly takes the year, month, and day as arguments.

Example:
```smalltalk
| aDate |
aDate := Date year: 2023 month: 10 day: 5.
```
This will create a `Date` object representing October 5, 2023.

### Creating a `DateAndTime` Object

Similarly, to create a `DateAndTime` object, you can use the `DateAndTime class>>year:month:day:hour:minute:second:offset:` method. For simplicity, if you only need to set the date part, you can use the `DateAndTime class>>year:month:day:` method, which defaults the time to midnight and the offset to the local time zone.

Example:
```smalltalk
| aDateTime |
aDateTime := DateAndTime year: 2023 month: 10 day: 5.
```
This will create a `DateAndTime` object representing October 5, 2023, at midnight in the local time zone.

### Summary

- **Date**: Use `Date year:month:day:` to create a `Date` object.
- **DateAndTime**: Use `DateAndTime year:month:day:` to create a `DateAndTime` object.

These methods provide a straightforward way to set the day, month, and year values for `Date` and `DateAndTime` objects in Pharo.

References:
- Pharo By Example, Chapter on Date and Time handling【12:0†source】.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a `Dictionary` by its values in Smalltalk and obtain an `OrderedCollection` of the keys in descending order of their values, you can follow these steps:

1. **Extract the Associations**: Convert the `Dictionary` to a collection of its associations.
2. **Sort the Associations by Values**: Use a `SortedCollection` with a custom sort block to sort the associations by their values.
3. **Extract the Keys**: Collect the keys from the sorted associations into an `OrderedCollection`.

Here is a step-by-step explanation with code examples:

### Step 1: Extract the Associations
First, convert the `Dictionary` to a collection of its associations:
```smalltalk
| dict associations |
dict := Dictionary new.
dict at: 'Rankable1' put: 8.5.
dict at: 'Rankable2' put: 9.0.
associations := dict associations.
```

### Step 2: Sort the Associations by Values
Next, create a `SortedCollection` and provide a custom sort block to sort the associations by their values in descending order:
```smalltalk
| sortedAssociations |
sortedAssociations := associations asSortedCollection: [:a :b | a value > b value].
```
In this block, `a` and `b` are associations, and `a value > b value` ensures that the collection is sorted in descending order by the values.

### Step 3: Extract the Keys
Finally, collect the keys from the sorted associations into an `OrderedCollection`:
```smalltalk
| sortedKeys |
sortedKeys := sortedAssociations collect: [:assoc | assoc key].
```

### Complete Code Example
Combining all the steps, the complete code to achieve the desired result is:
```smalltalk
| dict associations sortedAssociations sortedKeys |
dict := Dictionary new.
dict at: 'Rankable1' put: 8.5.
dict at: 'Rankable2' put: 9.0.

associations := dict associations.
sortedAssociations := associations asSortedCollection: [:a :b | a value > b value].
sortedKeys := sortedAssociations collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

^ sortedKeys  "This will return an OrderedCollection('Rankable2' 'Rankable1')"
```

### Explanation
- **`dict associations`**: Converts the dictionary to a collection of associations.
- **`asSortedCollection: [:a :b | a value > b value]`**: Sorts the associations by their values in descending order.
- **`collect: [:assoc | assoc key]`**: Extracts the keys from the sorted associations into an `OrderedCollection`.

This approach leverages the flexibility of `SortedCollection` and custom sort blocks in Smalltalk to achieve the desired sorting by values【4:0†source】【4:4†source】【4:7†source】.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements in Pharo, you can use the `asSet` method to convert both arrays to sets and then compare these sets for equality. This approach works because sets inherently eliminate duplicate elements and do not consider the order of elements, making it a straightforward way to check for the same elements in both arrays.

Here is an example of how you can do this:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
```

In this example, `asSet` converts each array to a set, and then the `=` operator checks if the two sets are equal. If they are equal, it means both arrays contain the same elements, regardless of the order and duplicates.

### Explanation:
1. **Conversion to Set**: The `asSet` method converts the array to a set, which removes any duplicate elements and disregards the order of elements【6:6†source】.
2. **Equality Check**: The `=` operator is used to check if the two sets are equal. In Pharo, `=` tests for object equality, meaning it checks if the two sets contain the same elements【6:2†source】.

### Example:
```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(6 5 3 1).

(arr1 asSet = arr2 asSet) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
```
In this case, the result will be `true` because both arrays contain the same elements, even though the order is different.

### Handling Duplicates:
If you need to consider the number of occurrences of each element (i.e., the arrays should be considered the same only if they contain the same elements with the same frequencies), you can use `asBag` instead of `asSet`. A `Bag` is similar to a `Set` but allows duplicates:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6 6).
arr2 := #(6 5 3 1 6).

(arr1 asBag = arr2 asBag) ifTrue: [^ true] ifFalse: [^ false].
```
In this case, the result will be `true` because both arrays contain the same elements with the same frequencies【6:6†source】.

By using `asSet` or `asBag`, you can efficiently check if two arrays contain the same elements, either considering or ignoring the frequency of elements.
